section.title: The Writing on the Wall
section.sectionRationale: A generation after Nebuchadnezzar, his successor Belshazzar throws a drunken feast and desecrates the temple vessels to toast his idols. A disembodied hand writes four words on the wall, and only Daniel can read the verdict: the king has been weighed and found wanting, his kingdom numbered and finished. That very night Babylon falls. It is the sober counterpart to chapter 4, pride that will not learn, judged without reprieve.
section.thesis: The God who holds every ruler's breath in His hand weighs Belshazzar, who defied the Lord of heaven and would not humble himself as his father had, and finds him wanting; the writing on the wall announces a kingdom numbered, weighed, and divided, and that very night Babylon falls.
section.frameNote: Five beats: Belshazzar's feast and the desecration of the temple vessels; the terrifying hand and the wise men's failure; Daniel confronts the king's pride; the inscription read, numbered, weighed, divided; and Babylon falls that very night.

units.label: Belshazzar's feast and the temple vessels

verses.text: Later, King Belshazzar held a great feast for a thousand of his nobles, and he drank wine with them.

Under the influence of the wine, Belshazzar gave orders to bring in the gold and silver vessels that Nebuchadnezzar his father had taken from the temple in Jerusalem, so that the king could drink from them, along with his nobles, his wives, and his concubines.
Thus they brought in the gold vessels that had been taken from the temple, the house of God in Jerusalem, and the king drank from them, along with his nobles, his wives, and his concubines.
As they drank the wine, they praised their gods of gold and silver, bronze and iron, wood and stone.

units.label: The terrifying hand on the wall
units.note: the wise men cannot read it

verses.text: At that moment the fingers of a human hand appeared and wrote on the plaster of the wall, near the lampstand in the royal palace. As the king watched the hand that was writing,
his face grew pale and his thoughts so alarmed him that his hips gave way and his knees knocked together.

The king called out for the enchanters, astrologers, and diviners to be brought in, and he said to these wise men of Babylon, “Whoever reads this inscription and tells me its interpretation will be clothed in purple and have a gold chain placed around his neck, and he will be made the third highest ruler in the kingdom.”
So all the king's wise men came in, but they could not read the inscription or interpret it for him.
Then King Belshazzar became even more terrified, his face grew even more pale, and his nobles were bewildered.
Hearing the outcry of the king and his nobles, the queen entered the banquet hall. “O king, may you live forever!” she said. “Do not let your thoughts terrify you, or your face grow pale.

There is a man in your kingdom who has the spirit of the holy gods in him. In the days of your father he was found to have insight, intelligence, and wisdom like that of the gods. Your father, King Nebuchadnezzar, appointed him chief of the magicians, enchanters, astrologers, and diviners. Your own father, the king,

did this because Daniel, the one he named Belteshazzar, was found to have an extraordinary spirit, as well as knowledge, understanding, and the ability to interpret dreams, explain riddles, and solve difficult problems. Summon Daniel, therefore, and he will give you the interpretation.”

units.label: Daniel confronts the king's pride
units.note: you did not humble your heart

verses.text: So Daniel was brought before the king, who asked him, “Are you Daniel, one of the exiles my father the king brought from Judah?
I have heard that the spirit of the gods is in you, and that you have insight, intelligence, and extraordinary wisdom.
Now the wise men and enchanters were brought before me to read this inscription and interpret it for me, but they could not give its interpretation.

But I have heard about you, that you are able to give interpretations and solve difficult problems. Therefore, if you can read this inscription and give me its interpretation, you will be clothed in purple and have a gold chain placed around your neck, and you will be made the third highest ruler in the kingdom.”
In response, Daniel said to the king, “You may keep your gifts for yourself and give your rewards to someone else. Nevertheless, I will read the inscription for the king and interpret it for him.
As for you, O king, the Most High God gave your father Nebuchadnezzar sovereignty and greatness, glory and honor.

Because of the greatness that He bestowed on him, the people of every nation and language trembled in fear before him. He killed whom he wished and kept alive whom he wished; he exalted whom he wished and humbled whom he wished.
But when his heart became arrogant and his spirit was hardened with pride, he was deposed from his royal throne, and his glory was taken from him.

He was driven away from mankind, and his mind was like that of a beast. He lived with the wild donkeys and ate grass like an ox, and his body was drenched with the dew of heaven until he acknowledged that the Most High God rules over the kingdom of mankind, setting over it whom He wishes.
But you his son, O Belshazzar, have not humbled your heart, even though you knew all this.

Instead, you have exalted yourself against the {{mare_shemayya|Lord of heaven}}. The vessels from His house were brought to you, and as you drank wine from them with your nobles, wives, and concubines, you praised your gods of silver and gold, bronze and iron, wood and stone, which cannot see or hear or understand. But you have failed to glorify the God who holds in His hand your very breath and all your ways.
Therefore He sent the hand that wrote the inscription.
Now this is the inscription that was written: MENE, MENE, TEKEL, PARSIN.
And this is the interpretation of the message: {{mene|MENE}} means that God has numbered the days of your reign and brought it to an end.
{{tekel|TEKEL}} means that you have been weighed on the scales and found deficient.
{{peres|PERES}} means that your kingdom has been divided and given over to the Medes and Persians.”

units.label: Babylon falls that very night
units.note: the kingdom passes to the Medes

verses.text: Then Belshazzar gave the command, and they clothed Daniel in purple, placed a gold chain around his neck, and proclaimed him the third highest ruler in the kingdom.
That very night Belshazzar king of the Chaldeans was slain,
and Darius the Mede received the kingdom at the age of sixty-two.

mare_shemayya.gloss: The Lord of heaven. Daniel charges that Belshazzar has exalted himself against the Lord of heaven, the God who holds the king's very breath in His hand. The title exposes the folly of the feast: the man toasting idols with holy cups is defying the God who owns his next breath.

mene.gloss: Numbered. Mene sounds like the Aramaic for numbered; it means God has numbered the days of Belshazzar's reign and brought it to an end. The doubling, mene, mene, underlines the certainty of the verdict: the count is finished.

tekel.gloss: Weighed. Tekel sounds like the Aramaic for weighed; the king has been weighed on the scales and found deficient. It is the image of God's judgment testing a life and finding it wanting, a picture that runs throughout Scripture.

peres.gloss: Divided. Peres, the singular of Parsin, sounds like the Aramaic both for divided and for Persia; the kingdom is divided and handed to the Medes and Persians. In one word the verdict names both the sentence and the conqueror who will carry it out that very night.

basic.context: Belshazzar, ruling Babylon a generation after Nebuchadnezzar, throws a lavish drunken feast and, in defiance, drinks toasts to his idols from the sacred cups looted from God's temple. Suddenly a human hand appears and writes on the wall. Terrified, and with his wise men helpless, the king summons Daniel, who refuses the rewards, rebukes his pride, and reads the verdict: God has numbered, weighed, and divided his kingdom. That same night Babylon falls and Belshazzar is killed.
basic.hermeneutics: This narrative is the dark mirror of chapter 4. Nebuchadnezzar was humbled and restored; Belshazzar, who knew the story and refused to learn it, is judged without reprieve. The desecration of the temple vessels shows sin as open contempt for God. The message is that God patiently warns, but pride that will not humble itself finally meets a night when the account is settled.
basic.originalLanguages: Daniel says the king has defied the mare shemayya, the Lord of heaven, who holds his breath. The writing spells out the sentence in Aramaic terms: mene, numbered; tekel, weighed; peres, divided, a word that also sounds like Persia, naming the very conqueror at the gate.
basic.typology: The scales that weigh Belshazzar and find him wanting picture the judgment every person faces before God. Against that verdict, Christ is the one who was weighed and found perfect, and who bears the deficiency of His people, so that all who are in Him are not found wanting on the day of reckoning.
basic.christ: The writing on the wall is a preview of the final judgment, when every life is numbered and weighed. Belshazzar is found deficient; so would we all be. But Jesus stood in our place, fully righteous, and took our sentence, so that those who trust Him are covered by His worth. The God who holds our breath became flesh to give us His life.
basic.god: God is the Lord of heaven who holds every ruler's breath and all his ways in His hand. He is patient, giving warning after warning, but He is also just, and there comes a night when the numbered days run out. He humbles the proud who will not humble themselves, and He hands kingdoms to whom He wills.
basic.crossReferences: Proverbs 16 warns that pride precedes a fall. Galatians 6 says God is not mocked; we reap what we sow. And Hebrews 9 reminds us that it is appointed for man to die once, and after that the judgment, the reality behind the scales.
basic.oneStory: Babylon, the great enemy of God's people, falls in a single night, weighed and found wanting. The God who numbers the days of kingdoms is moving history toward the day when every account is settled and His own kingdom stands. For those in Christ, weighed and yet accepted in Him, that day is not terror but hope.

context.literary: The fourth court narrative, deliberately paired with chapter 4 as its grim counterpart: one proud king humbled and restored, the next proud king warned by that very example yet judged without mercy. The scene builds from feast to terror to verdict to sudden fall, closing the Babylonian era of the book.
context.historical: Belshazzar ruled as co-regent and effective king of Babylon while his father Nabonidus was away; this explains his offer to make Daniel the third ruler, since he himself was second. Babylon fell to the army of Cyrus the Persian in 539 BC, an event ancient sources describe as swift and nearly bloodless, matching the phrase that very night.
context.canonical: The desecration of the temple vessels ties back to chapter 1, where they were first carried off, and their abuse triggers judgment. The image of being weighed in the scales echoes the prophets and Job and looks forward to the final judgment. Babylon's fall becomes, in Revelation, a symbol of the collapse of every God-defying power.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that God patiently warns but justly judges proud rulers who defy Him, to contrast Belshazzar's willful arrogance with the lesson his father learned, and to assure God's people that the empire that plundered them will itself be weighed and fall.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes God's judgment on one specific king; it is not a formula for reading omens or predicting the fall of nations. What endures is the warning against pride and sacrilege, the certainty of divine judgment, and the call to humble ourselves before the God who holds our breath.

debates.question: Who is Darius the Mede who received the kingdom?

views.label: A throne name or governor under Cyrus
views.case: Since no king named Darius the Mede is otherwise attested at this moment, many identify him with a figure such as Gubaru, the governor Cyrus set over Babylon, or take Darius as a throne name or title for a ruler acting under Cyrus, harmonizing the account with the Persian conquest.

views.label: Another name for Cyrus himself
views.case: Some read Daniel 6:28 as saying Daniel prospered in the reign of Darius, that is, the reign of Cyrus, treating Darius the Mede and Cyrus the Persian as the same ruler under two names, a known ancient practice.

debates.bearing: The precise identification is a genuine historical question held with humility; either way the book's point stands, that Babylon fell exactly as God declared and the kingdom passed to the Medes and Persians.

study.originalLanguages: Daniel's indictment names the offense precisely: Belshazzar has lifted himself up against the mare shemayya, the Lord of heaven, the God who holds his very breath and all his ways in His hand, while praising gods of gold and stone that cannot see or hear. The verdict then comes in three loaded Aramaic words. Mene means numbered, and its doubling seals the certainty: the days are counted and finished. Tekel means weighed, the king tested on the scales and found deficient. Peres means divided, and because it also sounds like Persia, the single word both passes sentence and names the Medes and Persians who will execute it that very night.

typology.type: The king weighed on the scales and found wanting
typology.fulfillment: The judgment every person faces before God, from which only Christ, weighed and found perfect, can deliver, Romans 3:23-24.
God's vindication of what is holy, fulfilled in Christ who cleanses His temple and His people, John 2:19-21.

typology.type: Babylon fallen in a single night
typology.fulfillment: The collapse of every God-defying power at the last day, Babylon fallen in Revelation 18:2.

study.christ: The hand on the wall is a preview of the great assize, when every life is numbered and weighed in God's scales. Belshazzar is found deficient, and by that measure so would we all be, for all have sinned and fall short. But Jesus was weighed in the balance and found perfectly righteous, and He took upon Himself the sentence our deficiency deserves. All who are hidden in Him are not found wanting on the day of reckoning but are covered by His worth. The Lord of heaven who holds our breath in His hand took on flesh and breath Himself to give His life for ours.
study.god: God is the Lord of heaven, who gives kingdoms and takes them away, and in whose hand is the breath of every proud ruler. He is patient, granting warning upon warning, even the vivid example of Nebuchadnezzar's humbling for Belshazzar to heed. But He is also just, and His patience is not indifference; there is a night when the numbered days are spent and the account is settled. He humbles those who will not humble themselves.

crossReferences.note: Pride goes before destruction, enacted in Belshazzar's fall the very night of his boast.
The prophet foretold Babylon's rulers falling in the midst of their feasting, fulfilled in this chapter.
God is not mocked; a man reaps what he sows, the principle behind the writing on the wall.
Fallen, fallen is Babylon the Great, the final collapse of every power that defies God.

study.oneStory: Babylon, the empire that plundered God's temple and enslaved His people, is weighed in the balance and falls in a single night. The God who numbers the days of kingdoms is steering history to the day when every account is settled and His own unshakable kingdom stands. Belshazzar's terror at the scales is the terror of the last judgment; but for those found in Christ, weighed and yet accepted because of His righteousness, that day is not dread but hope, for the Lord of heaven who holds our breath has given us His life.

quiz.q: What act of defiance marked Belshazzar's feast?

options.text: He drank toasts to his idols from the sacred vessels looted from God's temple in Jerusalem.
options.note: Daniel 5:2-4 shows the deliberate sacrilege that provokes the judgment.

options.text: He simply held a large banquet with much wine.
options.note: The feast was real, but the offense was desecrating the holy temple vessels to praise idols.

options.text: He refused to invite the wise men of Babylon.
options.note: The wise men were summoned later; the offense was the sacrilege with the vessels.

options.text: He tore down the temple in Jerusalem.
options.note: The temple had been plundered earlier; here he abuses the vessels already taken.

quiz.q: Why was Belshazzar more guilty than Nebuchadnezzar had been?

options.text: He knew how God had humbled his father, yet still refused to humble his own heart.
options.note: Daniel 5:22 makes his knowledge the aggravating factor.

options.text: He committed more feasts than his father.
options.note: The issue was not the number of feasts but refusing to learn from a known warning.

options.text: He was a foreigner and did not know the true God.
options.note: He did know the story of Nebuchadnezzar, which is why his pride was worse.

options.text: He had never heard of the God of Israel.
options.note: Daniel says he knew all this, so his defiance was willful.

quiz.q: What did the words on the wall mean?

options.text: Numbered, weighed, and divided: God had ended his reign, found him wanting, and given his kingdom to the Medes and Persians.
options.note: Daniel 5:26-28 gives the threefold interpretation.

options.text: A blessing promising a long and prosperous reign.
options.note: The opposite; the words announced judgment and the end of his reign.

options.text: A riddle with no clear meaning.
options.note: Daniel read it plainly as a verdict of judgment.

options.text: A list of the king's treasures.
options.note: They were terms of judgment: numbered, weighed, divided.

quiz.q: How did Daniel respond to the king's offer of rewards?

options.text: He told the king to keep the gifts, then read and interpreted the writing anyway.
options.note: Daniel 5:17 shows his integrity, unbought by the king's rewards.

options.text: He eagerly accepted the purple robe and gold chain first.
options.note: He declined the rewards before speaking, refusing to be bought.

options.text: He refused to speak unless paid double.
options.note: He asked for nothing and spoke the truth freely.

options.text: He fled the palace in fear.
options.note: He stayed and boldly delivered God's verdict.

quiz.q: How does being weighed and found wanting point us to Christ?

options.text: All fall short in God's scales, but Christ was found perfect and bears our deficiency, so those in Him are not condemned.
options.note: The scales picture judgment; Christ is our righteousness, Romans 3:23-24.

options.text: It teaches that good deeds will always outweigh bad ones.
options.note: The point is that we are all found wanting; our hope is Christ's righteousness, not our balance of deeds.

options.text: It shows that only kings are judged by God.
options.note: The scales picture the judgment all people face, not only kings.

options.text: It has no bearing on Christ or salvation.
options.note: The scene previews the judgment from which only Christ can deliver.

questions: Belshazzar treated holy things with contempt to entertain his guests. Where might you be treating what belongs to God casually or carelessly?
He knew the story of his father's humbling but refused to learn from it. What lessons has God already put before you that you are slow to take to heart?
The God who holds your very breath is the one Belshazzar defied. How does remembering that every breath is a gift from God reshape your day?
Daniel could not be bought; he spoke the truth whatever the reward. Where are you tempted to soften God's truth for the sake of approval or gain?
The scales of judgment would find any of us wanting. How does resting in the righteousness of Christ change the way you face the thought of God's judgment?

translationNotes.note: The Aramaic does not include Later; about thirty years have passed since chapter 4, so some translations add many years later to mark the gap.
Father can mean predecessor, ancestor, or grandfather; Belshazzar was a successor of Nebuchadnezzar, ruling as co-regent under his father Nabonidus.
The four words, mene, mene, tekel, parsin, are Aramaic terms that sound like numbered, weighed, and divided; peres, the singular of parsin, also sounds like Persia.
Darius the Mede is variously identified with a governor set over Babylon by Cyrus, or with Cyrus himself under another name; the historical question is held with humility.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Daniel 5:1-31. No em or en dashes appeared in the source verses. Chapter 5 is Aramaic, so load-bearing lexicon entries use Aramaic. No em or en dashes used.
